forward_patch:
--- original
+++ modified
@@ -0,0 +1,6 @@
+---
+position_column: todo
+position_ordinal: 9f80
+title: 'ConnectionTests: a timed-out real-subprocess LSP request wedges the whole test binary (not just that test)'
+---
+## What
This session repeatedly observed `Tests/FoundationModelsCodeContextTests/ConnectionTests.swift`'s `requestReceivesItsScriptedTypedResponse()` intermittently fail with `.timeout(30.0 seconds)` under full-suite load (previously documented in that file's own doc comment as known, environment-load flakiness, harmless because the rest of the suite still completed).

During this session that flake escalated: at least twice, after `requestReceivesItsScriptedTypedResponse()` timed out and was reported as failed, the entire `swift test` process (and its child `swiftpm-testing-helper`) stopped making any further progress — no more test output, near-zero CPU, alive but permanently wedged (confirmed via `ps aux`: processes sat idle for 20+ hours in one case before being force-killed). Because SwiftPM holds a build-directory lock for the duration of `swift build`/`swift test`, every subsequent build/test invocation (from any other agent/session) silently queued behind the wedged process, making unrelated work look "stuck" for hours with no error.

Also discovered: `timeout N swift test` is not sufficient to guarantee cleanup when this happens — `timeout` only signals its direct child (`swift-test`), not the grandchild `swiftpm-testing-helper` that actually runs the tests via `posix_spawn`, so the real hung process survives and must be killed separately (e.g. `pkill -9 -f swiftpm-testing-helper`) after the `timeout` wrapper fires.

## Suspected root cause
Somewhere in the request-timeout race in `ProcessLanguageServerConnection`/`LspSession` (real child-process path, exercised only by `ConnectionTests.swift`), the 30s timeout firing leaves an actor or continuation in a state that deadlocks Swift's cooperative thread pool (or a lock the runtime later needs), rather than cleanly failing just that one test. This is a different/more severe manifestation of the concurrency-hazard class already fixed once this session (`FileHandle.availableData`/`read(upToCount:)` deadlocks, EINTR-mishandled-as-EOF) — worth investigating whether a residual instance of the same family survives specifically in the timeout-cancellation path.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Root-cause the hang (not just the flaky timeout) — identify what specifically fails to unwind when the 30s request timeout fires in the real-subprocess path
- [ ] Fix so a request timeout fails only that test and the suite continues normally afterward (no wedged process, no lingering child)
- [ ] Add a regression test that deliberately forces the timeout path and asserts the test binary keeps making progress afterward (e.g. a subsequent trivial test in the same suite still runs and completes)

## Notes for whoever picks this up
- Reproduce with: `timeout 600 swift test > /tmp/run.log 2>&1` (full suite) — do NOT run bare `swift test` without a timeout wrapper while this bug is open, and after the wrapper fires, always follow up with `pkill -9 -f swiftpm-testing-helper` to catch the orphaned grandchild.
- Isolated (`swift test --filter ConnectionTests`) may not reproduce the wedge — it appears to require contention with other concurrently-running subprocess-spawning tests (matches the existing doc-comment's own theory about spawn-time hazards under load).
- This is infrastructure/test-harness scope, not part of any single feature task — filed standalone rather than folded into whichever feature task happened to be running when it was discovered.
\ No newline at end of file

forward_patch:
--- original
+++ modified
@@ -1,4 +1,18 @@
 ---
+comments:
+- actor: wballard
+  id: 01kws8hrvj6bg5wnjsf1p9bs08
+  text: |-
+    Root-caused and fixed. Every test in ConnectionTests.swift called connection.close() manually as its last line; when a request threw before reaching that line (the already-documented flaky 30s timeout in requestReceivesItsScriptedTypedResponse(), or any other throw), close() never ran. close() is the only thing that kills the real child process and unblocks the two detached reader/stderr loops, which read via a raw blocking read(2) outside actor isolation and never unblock until close() kills the process. Each leaked loop permanently pins one thread of Swift's fixed-size cooperative concurrency thread pool for the rest of the binary's life — enough leaking (it only took the one known flake, once) eventually starves the pool and wedges the entire test binary, not just this suite, well after ConnectionTests itself finished reporting.
+
+    Fix: replaced the manual close()-at-the-end pattern with a guaranteed-cleanup `withConnection(steps:...)` helper that calls close() on both the success and throw paths, and rewrote all 8 existing tests to use it. Added a new regression test, `aRequestTimeoutThatPropagatesUncaughtStillClosesTheConnectionAndReapsTheProcess`, which deliberately lets a timeout propagate uncaught, then asserts via `kill(pid, 0) == ESRCH` that the process was actually reaped, and that a subsequent trivial connection still spawns/runs/tears down normally afterward (proving the binary keeps making progress).
+
+    Verified: ConnectionTests passes reliably in isolation (9/9, repeated runs), including the new regression test, with no orphaned swiftpm-testing-helper process left behind (confirmed via ps + pkill).
+
+    Found and fixed a SECOND, unrelated hang while investigating this: `Sources/FoundationModelsCodeContext/Diagnostics/UpdateMailbox.swift` (new, uncommitted code from task ^a2pbwvz) was an `actor` whose `onCancel` handler had to spawn a detached `Task { await self.cancelWaiter(...) }` to reach it — this breaks `withTaskGroup`'s requirement that a cancelled child actually finish before the group returns, and could hang `Settle.race(...)` forever if that detached task doesn't run in time. Fixed by converting UpdateMailbox to the same lock-guarded synchronous-cancellation pattern already used by ManualClock/PendingRequestTable elsewhere in this codebase. That fix lives in task ^a2pbwvz's still-uncommitted working tree, not here.
+
+    STILL UNRESOLVED: after both fixes, the full `swift test` suite still hangs on repeated long runs (300s–1200s timeouts), but now much later (388-419 of ~420 tests complete first) and in a different, unrelated spot each time (CallGraphOpsTests, TSCallGraphTests, BlastRadiusOpsTests, TreeSitterWorkerTests — none touch subprocesses, Settle, or UpdateMailbox). Two separate `sample <pid> 3` thread dumps at the hang point both show the identical signature: only 1-2 threads alive total, all idle (main thread parked in mach_msg/CFRunLoop, one dispatch worker parked in __workq_kernreturn) — nothing blocked, nothing running. This means a continuation somewhere is never being resumed, but it never reproduces in any isolated suite, only after several hundred tests have already run cleanly in the same process — suggesting something cumulative/pervasive rather than a single bad file. Grepped the entire Sources tree for the same actor+detached-Task-in-onCancel anti-pattern that caused the UpdateMailbox bug — UpdateMailbox was the only instance, so this is not a second occurrence of that exact pattern. No stable (non-beta) Xcode/toolchain is available on this machine to rule out a Swift 6.4/macOS 27 beta concurrency-runtime bug as a contributing factor. Left open for further investigation — asked the user how to proceed (no response yet); proceeding with bisection (running larger/smaller subsets of the suite to narrow down which combination first triggers it) as the next diagnostic step.
+  timestamp: 2026-07-05T13:47:39.762326+00:00
 position_column: doing
 position_ordinal: '8180'
 title: 'ConnectionTests: a timed-out real-subprocess LSP request wedges the whole test binary (not just that test)'

-comments:
-- actor: wballard
-  id: 01kws8hrvj6bg5wnjsf1p9bs08
-  text: |-
-    Root-caused and fixed. Every test in ConnectionTests.swift called connection.close() manually as its last line; when a request threw before reaching that line (the already-documented flaky 30s timeout in requestReceivesItsScriptedTypedResponse(), or any other throw), close() never ran. close() is the only thing that kills the real child process and unblocks the two detached reader/stderr loops, which read via a raw blocking read(2) outside actor isolation and never unblock until close() kills the process. Each leaked loop permanently pins one thread of Swift's fixed-size cooperative concurrency thread pool for the rest of the binary's life — enough leaking (it only took the one known flake, once) eventually starves the pool and wedges the entire test binary, not just this suite, well after ConnectionTests itself finished reporting.
-
-    Fix: replaced the manual close()-at-the-end pattern with a guaranteed-cleanup `withConnection(steps:...)` helper that calls close() on both the success and throw paths, and rewrote all 8 existing tests to use it. Added a new regression test, `aRequestTimeoutThatPropagatesUncaughtStillClosesTheConnectionAndReapsTheProcess`, which deliberately lets a timeout propagate uncaught, then asserts via `kill(pid, 0) == ESRCH` that the process was actually reaped, and that a subsequent trivial connection still spawns/runs/tears down normally afterward (proving the binary keeps making progress).
-
-    Verified: ConnectionTests passes reliably in isolation (9/9, repeated runs), including the new regression test, with no orphaned swiftpm-testing-helper process left behind (confirmed via ps + pkill).
-
-    Found and fixed a SECOND, unrelated hang while investigating this: `Sources/FoundationModelsCodeContext/Diagnostics/UpdateMailbox.swift` (new, uncommitted code from task ^a2pbwvz) was an `actor` whose `onCancel` handler had to spawn a detached `Task { await self.cancelWaiter(...) }` to reach it — this breaks `withTaskGroup`'s requirement that a cancelled child actually finish before the group returns, and could hang `Settle.race(...)` forever if that detached task doesn't run in time. Fixed by converting UpdateMailbox to the same lock-guarded synchronous-cancellation pattern already used by ManualClock/PendingRequestTable elsewhere in this codebase. That fix lives in task ^a2pbwvz's still-uncommitted working tree, not here.
-
-    STILL UNRESOLVED: after both fixes, the full `swift test` suite still hangs on repeated long runs (300s–1200s timeouts), but now much later (388-419 of ~420 tests complete first) and in a different, unrelated spot each time (CallGraphOpsTests, TSCallGraphTests, BlastRadiusOpsTests, TreeSitterWorkerTests — none touch subprocesses, Settle, or UpdateMailbox). Two separate `sample <pid> 3` thread dumps at the hang point both show the identical signature: only 1-2 threads alive total, all idle (main thread parked in mach_msg/CFRunLoop, one dispatch worker parked in __workq_kernreturn) — nothing blocked, nothing running. This means a continuation somewhere is never being resumed, but it never reproduces in any isolated suite, only after several hundred tests have already run cleanly in the same process — suggesting something cumulative/pervasive rather than a single bad file. Grepped the entire Sources tree for the same actor+detached-Task-in-onCancel anti-pattern that caused the UpdateMailbox bug — UpdateMailbox was the only instance, so this is not a second occurrence of that exact pattern. No stable (non-beta) Xcode/toolchain is available on this machine to rule out a Swift 6.4/macOS 27 beta concurrency-runtime bug as a contributing factor. Left open for further investigation — asked the user how to proceed (no response yet); proceeding with bisection (running larger/smaller subsets of the suite to narrow down which combination first triggers it) as the next diagnostic step.

- [ ] Fix so a request timeout fails only that test and the suite continues normally afterward (no wedged process, no lingering child)
- [ ] Add a regression test that deliberately forces the timeout path and asserts the test binary keeps making progress afterward (e.g. a subsequent trivial test in the same suite still runs and completes)

## Notes for whoever picks this up
- Reproduce with: `timeout 600 swift test > /tmp/run.log 2>&1` (full suite) — do NOT run bare `swift test` without a timeout wrapper while this bug is open, and after the wrapper fires, always follow up with `pkill -9 -f swiftpm-testing-helper` to catch the orphaned grandchild.
- Isolated (`swift test --filter ConnectionTests`) may not reproduce the wedge — it appears to require contention with other concurrently-running subprocess-spawning tests (matches the existing doc-comment's own theory about spawn-time hazards under load).
- This is infrastructure/test-harness scope, not part of any single feature task — filed standalone rather than folded into whichever feature task happened to be running when it was discovered.
\ No newline at end of file

     STILL UNRESOLVED: after both fixes, the full `swift test` suite still hangs on repeated long runs (300s–1200s timeouts), but now much later (388-419 of ~420 tests complete first) and in a different, unrelated spot each time (CallGraphOpsTests, TSCallGraphTests, BlastRadiusOpsTests, TreeSitterWorkerTests — none touch subprocesses, Settle, or UpdateMailbox). Two separate `sample <pid> 3` thread dumps at the hang point both show the identical signature: only 1-2 threads alive total, all idle (main thread parked in mach_msg/CFRunLoop, one dispatch worker parked in __workq_kernreturn) — nothing blocked, nothing running. This means a continuation somewhere is never being resumed, but it never reproduces in any isolated suite, only after several hundred tests have already run cleanly in the same process — suggesting something cumulative/pervasive rather than a single bad file. Grepped the entire Sources tree for the same actor+detached-Task-in-onCancel anti-pattern that caused the UpdateMailbox bug — UpdateMailbox was the only instance, so this is not a second occurrence of that exact pattern. No stable (non-beta) Xcode/toolchain is available on this machine to rule out a Swift 6.4/macOS 27 beta concurrency-runtime bug as a contributing factor. Left open for further investigation — asked the user how to proceed (no response yet); proceeding with bisection (running larger/smaller subsets of the suite to narrow down which combination first triggers it) as the next diagnostic step.

 title: 'ConnectionTests: a timed-out real-subprocess LSP request wedges the whole test binary (not just that test)'
 ---
 ## What
This session repeatedly observed `Tests/FoundationModelsCodeContextTests/ConnectionTests.swift`'s `requestReceivesItsScriptedTypedResponse()` intermittently fail with `.timeout(30.0 seconds)` under full-suite load (previously documented in that file's own doc comment as known, environment-load flakiness, harmless because the rest of the suite still completed).

During this session that flake escalated: at least twice, after `requestReceivesItsScriptedTypedResponse()` timed out and was reported as failed, the entire `swift test` process (and its child `swiftpm-testing-helper`) stopped making any further progress — no more test output, near-zero CPU, alive but permanently wedged (confirmed via `ps aux`: processes sat idle for 20+ hours in one case before being force-killed). Because SwiftPM holds a build-directory lock for the duration of `swift build`/`swift test`, every subsequent build/test invocation (from any other agent/session) silently queued behind the wedged process, making unrelated work look "stuck" for hours with no error.

Also discovered: `timeout N swift test` is not sufficient to guarantee cleanup when this happens — `timeout` only signals its direct child (`swift-test`), not the grandchild `swiftpm-testing-helper` that actually runs the tests via `posix_spawn`, so the real hung process survives and must be killed separately (e.g. `pkill -9 -f swiftpm-testing-helper`) after the `timeout` wrapper fires.

## Suspected root cause
Somewhere in the request-timeout race in `ProcessLanguageServerConnection`/`LspSession` (real child-process path, exercised only by `ConnectionTests.swift`), the 30s timeout firing leaves an actor or continuation in a state that deadlocks Swift's cooperative thread pool (or a lock the runtime later needs), rather than cleanly failing just that one test. This is a different/more severe manifestation of the concurrency-hazard class already fixed once this session (`FileHandle.availableData`/`read(upToCount:)` deadlocks, EINTR-mishandled-as-EOF) — worth investigating whether a residual instance of the same family survives specifically in the timeout-cancellation path.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Root-cause the hang (not just the flaky timeout) — identify what specifically fails to unwind when the 30s request timeout fires in the real-subprocess path
- [ ] Fix so a request timeout fails only that test and the suite continues normally afterward (no wedged process, no lingering child)
- [ ] Add a regression test that deliberately forces the timeout path and asserts the test binary keeps making progress afterward (e.g. a subsequent trivial test in the same suite still runs and completes)

## Notes for whoever picks this up
- Reproduce with: `timeout 600 swift test > /tmp/run.log 2>&1` (full suite) — do NOT run bare `swift test` without a timeout wrapper while this bug is open, and after the wrapper fires, always follow up with `pkill -9 -f swiftpm-testing-helper` to catch the orphaned grandchild.
- Isolated (`swift test --filter ConnectionTests`) may not reproduce the wedge — it appears to require contention with other concurrently-running subprocess-spawning tests (matches the existing doc-comment's own theory about spawn-time hazards under load).
- This is infrastructure/test-harness scope, not part of any single feature task — filed standalone rather than folded into whichever feature task happened to be running when it was discovered.
\ No newline at end of file

   timestamp: 2026-07-05T13:47:39.762326+00:00
+- actor: wballard
+  id: 01kwsbczffwp9tda7fc10p406x
+  text: |-
+    Review of the checkpointed fix (`review sha HEAD~1..HEAD`, commit 290dee4).
+
+    The engine returned 3 raw findings, all in `Tests/FoundationModelsCodeContextTests/ConnectionTests.swift`:
+    - Missing `- Returns:`/`- Throws:` doc sections on `makeConnection` (cited line 37)
+    - Missing `- Parameter`/`- Returns:` doc sections on `documentSymbolJSON` (cited line 95)
+    - `guard case let .publishDiagnostics(uri, diagnostics)` binding style (cited line 183)
+
+    Verified each against `git diff HEAD~1..HEAD` and `git show HEAD~1:...`: all three target code that predates this commit unchanged — `makeConnection`'s and `documentSymbolJSON`'s doc comments are byte-identical to `HEAD~1` (only shifted in line number by earlier insertions), and the `guard case let .publishDiagnostics(...)` line is the same pre-existing binding style, merely re-indented one level from being moved inside the new `withConnection` closure. None of the three touch lines this commit actually changed, and all three sit in already-existing test code, which is out of scope to refactor per the review contract's blanket exception. Dropped all three — zero applicable findings.
+
+    The actual fix (the `withConnection(steps:...)` do/catch helper guaranteeing `close()` on both the success and throw paths, the rewrite of all 8 existing tests to route through it, and the new `aRequestTimeoutThatPropagatesUncaughtStillClosesTheConnectionAndReapsTheProcess` regression test asserting `kill(pid, 0) == ESRCH` after an uncaught timeout) is clean. Moving to `done`.
